title: A Cryogenic Uniaxial Strain Cell for Quantum Devices
authors: Bradley Lloyd, Davis Rash, Chandler Wilburn, Paul Kliewer, Meenakshi Singh
abstract: Mechanical strain is a powerful resource for tuning quantum systems, but existing piezoelectric strain cells are generally optimized for fragile, high-aspect-ratio single crystals rather than the thick, square-profile chips typical of semiconductor quantum devices. Furthermore, adapting these cells for qubits requires accommodating dense RF and DC wiring while maintaining strict electrical isolation from high-voltage piezo actuators. Here, we present a piezoelectric uniaxial strain cell designed to homogeneously strain thick, square-profile substrates. We introduce a highly symmetric dual-chip loading configuration that effectively suppresses flexural deformation and shear stress. The cell integrates a high-density RF/DC interposer to support standard wire bonding and encloses the actuators in a grounded Faraday cage to prevent unwanted Stark shifts in the device layer. Finite element simulations confirm that combining stiff actuators with this symmetric mounting drastically improves strain homogeneity. Finally, we validate the apparatus experimentally by applying uniaxial strain to a 200 $μ$m thick silicon die. Surface strain measurements demonstrate an applied strain of 215 $με$ for 200 V applied piezo bias.
summary: The paper introduces a novel cryogenic uniaxial strain cell specifically engineered to apply uniform mechanical strain to thick, square semiconductor chips. This design overcomes limitations of prior cells by integrating high-density electrical wiring and using advanced shielding techniques. It provides a critical tool for experimentally tuning quantum device properties via controlled strain at low temperatures.
main_problem: Existing piezoelectric strain cells are unsuitable for thick, square-profile semiconductor quantum devices, and integrating dense wiring while maintaining electrical isolation is challenging.
main_result: The authors designed a novel cryogenic uniaxial strain cell that homogeneously strains thick silicon dies while successfully incorporating high-density RF/DC interconnects and using Faraday cages for electrical isolation.
method: The design utilizes a highly symmetric dual-chip loading configuration with a three-stack actuation scheme, validated by finite element simulations and experimental measurement on a 200 $\mu$m thick silicon die.
model_or_system: The platform is a cryogenic uniaxial strain cell operating in a dilution refrigerator, designed to apply controlled mechanical strain to semiconductor quantum devices (e.g., Si qubits). The system incorporates complex electrical shielding and mounting structures.
key_observables: Applied uniaxial strain ($	ext{215 } \mu\epsilon$ for $200 	ext{V}$ bias), strain homogeneity ($\Delta$), and the ability to support dense RF/DC wiring under strain.
important_parameters: Cryogenic temperatures (down to 50 mK), applied voltage ($\pm 200 	ext{V}$), substrate thickness ($200 \mu	ext{m}$).
main_assumptions: The symmetric mounting configuration effectively suppresses flexural deformation and shear stress, and the Faraday cage successfully prevents unwanted Stark shifts.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides an exploded overview of the strain cell design components. Figure 2 details measurements validating the setup's performance at cryogenic temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper first identifies limitations in current strain cells, then introduces the novel symmetric dual-chip loading design and electrical integration strategy. It presents simulation results confirming homogeneity improvements before concluding with experimental validation on a silicon die.
why_interesting: This work provides essential hardware infrastructure for realizing quantum devices whose properties are tunable by mechanical strain, which is crucial for many solid-state qubit platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 2. Strain measured by the change in resistance of a 1.5 × 1.0 mm2 strain gauge epoxied to the center of the dorsal sample, showing a linear relationship between strain and voltage applied to the piezo stacks. Inset: assembled strain cell mounted to the dilution refrigerator puck.

Figure 3. Finite element analysis of axial strain (εxx) (a) Three-dimensional strain map of the dorsal silicon die under 200 V differential piezo bias at 50 mK, showing the distribution of axial strain across the chip surface for the asymmetrically loaded (top) and symmetrically loaded (bottom) cases. (b) Axial strain at the center of the 1×1 mm² region of interest as a function of substrate thickness for three mounting configurations: symmetric dual-chip loading (circles), asymmetric single-chip loading (squares), and chip epoxied directly to the actuator face (triangles).
